title: Zombicide Invader - Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UZ3583GSSOo

Daniel (@dungeondive) delivers his verdict on **Zombicide: Invader**, and it's also his farewell to the franchise. After repeatedly hoping each new box would make him love Zombicide — a bit of sunk-cost fallacy, he admits — he's decided the series simply isn't for him and is selling off his collection (though he may keep the minis for Warhammer Quest and Core Space).

He's careful to separate taste from quality: the rules are clear and concise, the production and dashboards are impeccable, and the XP-driven skill unlocks add genuine variety. His problems are personal. The corridor-crawl movement bores him; the maps are pure art with nothing to interact with — no traps, no events — and the six-character requirement is a real burden solo, forcing constant rulebook checks across too many powers. Shrinking the party would wreck the game's tight health-pool balance.

He does praise Invader's additions: the *sustained fire* mechanic (pooling dice in an all-or-nothing press-your-luck attack to fell abominations) is a highlight, and the spreading mold/spore abominations add a wrinkle. But it lands in a middle ground — too fiddly to be a pure chaotic-combat rush, too thin to be truly interesting. A well-made game, he concludes, for people who aren't him.

----

Daniel finally quit a well-made game purely because it didn't click with how he plays. When have you let go of an objectively good game that just wasn't for you?
